plan.contextChunk:
are, essentially, inverses of each other. Many algorithms explicitly
treat them as inverse problems, where solving the problem in one
direction can inform the solution in the other direction.

In @sec-neural_nets, we described neural nets as being a
sequence of mappings from raw data to ever more abstracted
representations, layer by layer. This perspective puts representation
learning in the spotlight: deep learning is just representation
learning! Let us now point out an alternative perspective: in backward
order, deep nets are mappings from abstracted representations to ever
more concrete representations of the data, layer by layer. This backward
ordering is the direction in which deep generative networks work. This
perspective puts the spotlight on generative modeling: deep learning is
just generative modeling! Indeed both modeling directions are valid, and
the full picture looks like this
(@fig-generative_modeling_and_representation_learning-rep_gen_schematic):

![The relationship between representation learning and generative modeling. Here we label one side as "Data" and the other as "Embedding"; but what's the precise difference between these two things? Why is an RGB image data while a 100-dimensional vector of neural activations is an embedding? This is a question for you to think about; there is no correct answer.](./figures/generative_modeling_and_representation_learning/rep_gen_schematic.png){width="35%" #fig-generative_modeling_and_representation_learning-rep_gen_schematic}

:::{.column-margin}
Moving backward through a net is also what backpropagation does, but it computes a different function: backpropagation computes the gradient $\nabla f$ whereas here we focus on the inverse $f^{-1}$.
:::



## Latent Variables as Representations

In @sec-generative_models, we introduced generative models
with latent variables $\mathbf{z}$. In that context, the role of the
latent variable was to specify all unobserved factors that might affect
the output of a model. For example, if the model predicts the color of a
black and white photo, it is a mapping
$g: \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{z} \rightarrow \mathbf{y}$, with $\mathbf{x}$

interactions.label: Flow Direction
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates the bidirectional nature of deep networks: bottom-up for encoding/representation learning, and top-down for decoding/generative modeling.

interactions.label: Inspect Layer
interactions.teaches: Highlights a specific slice in the network hierarchy (from 0=Data to 4=Embedding), demonstrating how raw data is iteratively mapped into abstract latent features.

demo_steps.title: Two Sides of One Coin
demo_steps.narration: Deep learning models can be viewed in two directions. From bottom to top, representation learning maps concrete, high-dimensional data to abstract, low-dimensional embeddings. In reverse, generative models map those abstract embeddings back into realistic data.

demo_steps.title: Representation Learning
demo_steps.narration: Watch the particles flow upwards from the Data layer. As they pass through each intermediate layer, the raw input coordinate is processed and transformed, converging into organized semantic codes at the Embedding layer.

demo_steps.title: Generative Modeling
demo_steps.narration: Now look at the inverse direction. By starting with a vector in the latent embedding space and mapping it downwards, the generative model reconstructs a complex data sample. The paths are exactly reversed, showing how generator and encoder are physical inverses.

demo_steps.title: Intermediate Feature Spaces
demo_steps.narration: Select middle layers to inspect. Every horizontal plane represents a layer's feature representation space. The curved paths illustrate how neighborhood relationships warp and simplify as you ascend the network hierarchy.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: An angled isometric view looking slightly down on the stack of planes, showing the clear vertical separation of the layers and the curvature of the connecting lines.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) The dominant vertical edge of the cuboid runs straight up and down in the source image, with the layers stacked vertically. (2) The horizontal square planes are skewed symmetrically, with edges receding at equal angles to the left-down and right-down directions. This symmetry suggests the camera is positioned directly broadside to the corner, corresponding to an azimuth of 45 degrees. (3) The top surfaces of all horizontal slices are clearly visible, indicating a shallow downward tilt (elevation of around 20-30 degrees) to resolve the depth of each individual plane.

camera_view.view_notes: The 45-degree azimuth aligns perfectly with the symmetric corner projection of the source diagram. The low elevation angle of 25 degrees matches the foreshortening of the horizontal layer planes, allowing labels on both side axes to remain fully readable.

inline_constraints.default_view: Must show the stacked planes inside a transparent 3D wireframe box, matching the vertical arrows on the sides and the 'Data'/'Embedding' text placement.
inline_constraints.visible_ui: A flow direction toggle and a layer inspection slider placed non-intrusively near the right or bottom margin.
inline_constraints.explainers: Hovering over any horizontal plane reveals its layer index and its role as a feature manifold; hovering over a path displays its corresponding data-to-embedding trajectory.
inline_constraints.framing: Maintain vertical space to accommodate the 'Representation learning' and 'Generative modeling' side arrows without cluttering the main 3D canvas.
inline_constraints.camera: Align perfectly with the original isometric-like orthographic angle so the visual transition from static PDF image to interactive 3D is seamless.

interactionPlan.notes: Represent the curved mapping lines as quadratic or cubic Bezier curves in 3D space starting at the bottom dots and ending at the top arrowheads. The animated particles should glide along these curves, with their flow direction reversing when 'flowDirection' changes. The currently selected layer plane should glow slightly or have its wireframe border highlighted.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a 3D wireframe cuboid with horizontal planes, viewed from an elevated orthographic perspective (about 45 degrees azimuth and 25 degrees elevation). The vertical edges of the cuboid are clearly defined, and the entire structure is centered with vertical labels on the far left and right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot matches the elevated orthographic perspective of the source closely. However, the side arrows and side labels are almost entirely clipped or missing from the frame, and the top/bottom labels overlap directly with the wireframe geometry.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: While the camera perspective of the main cuboid is highly accurate, the framing and scale of peripheral elements (like the side arrows and rotated text labels) are poorly adjusted, causing them to be clipped out of the viewport.
discrepancies: The 'Embedding' and 'Data' labels overlap directly with the top and bottom wireframe edges of the cuboid.
The left and right side labels ('Representation learning' and 'Generative modeling') are completely missing or clipped from view.
The side vertical arrows are positioned too far from the cuboid and are mostly clipped out of the frame.
The bottom plane is colored yellow/beige by default due to active layer focus, whereas the original has uniform gray planes.
evaluation.notes: The main cuboid and curves are modeled well, but the labels overlap the geometry, and the side annotations/arrows are clipped and missing.
action_items: Increase the vertical spacing of 'Embedding' and 'Data' labels to prevent them from overlapping with the cube wireframe.
Bring the left and right vertical arrows closer to the cuboid (adjust their X coordinates) so they are fully visible within the viewport.
Ensure the rotated HTML side labels are correctly positioned next to the vertical arrows and visible on screen.
Default the selected layer focus to none (or -1) initially so that all planes are uniformly gray to match the static source image.
Adjust the Bezier control points to make the curves fan out more dramatically, matching the S-shape in the original diagram.

feedback.rationale: The concept and overall geometry are highly accurate (concept accuracy is 5/5), but concrete implementation details like label positioning, viewport clipping, and default state coloring need refinement by the generator.
action_items: Increase the vertical spacing of 'Embedding' and 'Data' labels to prevent them from overlapping with the cube wireframe.
Bring the left and right vertical arrows closer to the cuboid (adjust their X coordinates) so they are fully visible within the viewport.
Ensure the rotated HTML side labels are correctly positioned next to the vertical arrows and visible on screen.
Default the selected layer focus to none (or -1) initially so that all planes are uniformly gray to match the static source image.
Adjust the Bezier control points to make the curves fan out more dramatically, matching the S-shape in the original diagram.

Adjust the Bezier control points to make the curves fan out more dramatically, matching the S-shape in the original diagram.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a 3D bounding box (cuboid) containing stacked parallel horizontal planes, viewed from a slightly elevated angle looking down and to the left. The top face and two front-facing vertical sides of the cuboid are clearly visible, with perspective lines converging slightly.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the identical 3D cuboid with stacked horizontal planes, viewed from an elevated orthographic angle looking down and to the left. The top face and the two vertical sides are visible at a very similar orientation to the source.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles are highly consistent, with both presenting the cuboid at an isometric/orthographic elevation of around 25 degrees. The rendered camera matches the source's orientation very closely.

shape_comparison.rendered_shape: wireframe box constructed of thin cylinders
dashed curves made of small cylinders

discrepancies: Severe label collision: 'Embedding' and 'Data' HTML labels directly overlap the top and bottom edges of the cuboid geometry, unlike the original where they sit in clean white space.
The vertical labels 'Representation learning' and 'Generative modeling' are poorly positioned, overlapping the vertical arrows and the cuboid structure.
The vertical side arrows are positioned too close to the main cuboid compared to the spacious layout in the original diagram.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive implementation of the flow concept, but severely hampered by poorly positioned labels that collide with the 3D geometry.
action_items: Adjust the vertical position (offset/translation) of 'Embedding' and 'Data' labels so they sit completely outside the 3D cuboid bounding box.
Move the left and right side arrows (and their corresponding rotated labels) further outward along the X-axis to match the spaciousness of the original figure.
Tune the CSS or offset of the rotated vertical labels to ensure they do not clip or overlap with the 3D arrow shafts.
Reduce the size of the HTML labels slightly to prevent visual clutter in the canvas.

feedback.rationale: The 3D interactive concept is highly accurate, but the implementation has several visual bugs including overlapping labels, tight margins, and poor positioning of side arrows, which can be resolved by refining the generator execution.

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a 3D cuboid frame with horizontal planes stacked vertically, viewed from an elevated orthographic isometric angle (down-left) with the front vertical edge closest to the viewer. There is ample white space around the main geometry for labels at the top, bottom, and sides.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot uses an orthographic projection from a similar isometric angle, but the camera is zoomed in too closely, leaving almost no vertical whitespace.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: While the isometric perspective is mathematically similar, the framing is extremely tight, causing the top and bottom labels ('Embedding' and 'Data') to collide with and overlap the cuboid's wireframe edges.

shape_comparison.source_shape: gently curved dashed lines with arrowheads
long vertical line with single arrowhead

discrepancies: The 'Embedding' label overlaps with the top edge of the cuboid wireframe.
The 'Data' label is clipped at the bottom and overlaps with the bottom wireframe edge.
The cylinders used for the cuboid wireframe edges are too thick compared to the elegant, thin lines of the source.
The curves representing the mapping paths are too straight and vertical compared to the more expressive, sweeping curves in the source.
evaluation.notes: The 3D reconstruction is geometrically accurate and highly interactive, but suffers from poor framing, resulting in label overlaps and clipping.
action_items: Adjust the camera zoom or heightFraction in setCameraView to add more top and bottom margins, preventing label clipping and overlapping.
Reduce the radius of the cylinders forming the cuboid wireframe to make the structure look less bulky and more like clean lines.
Increase the lateral control point offsets of the CubicBezierCurve3 paths to give the curves a more pronounced, graceful bend.
Increase the offset of the 'Embedding' and 'Data' labels so they sit clearly outside the wireframe box boundaries.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are highly accurate, but execution-level adjustments are needed to fix camera framing, label overlaps, wireframe thickness, and curve shapes.

discrepancies: The 'Embedding' label overlaps with the top edge of the cuboid wireframe.
The 'Data' label is clipped at the bottom and overlaps with the bottom wireframe edge.
The cylinders used for the cuboid wireframe edges are too thick compared to the elegant, thin lines of the source.
The curves representing the mapping paths are too straight and vertical compared to the more expressive, sweeping curves in the source.
gemini-3.5-flash.notes: The 3D reconstruction is geometrically accurate and highly interactive, but suffers from poor framing, resulting in label overlaps and clipping.
action_items: Adjust the camera zoom or heightFraction in setCameraView to add more top and bottom margins, preventing label clipping and overlapping.
Reduce the radius of the cylinders forming the cuboid wireframe to make the structure look less bulky and more like clean lines.
Increase the lateral control point offsets of the CubicBezierCurve3 paths to give the curves a more pronounced, graceful bend.
Increase the offset of the 'Embedding' and 'Data' labels so they sit clearly outside the wireframe box boundaries.
